issue_summary: Treat separator at end of filename as sign of directory Adjust other methods to this new definition Adjust absolute prefix calculation to always end in separator Add new methods that omit the end separator
javadoc_before:
/** * Utility class that provides methods to manipulate filenames and filepaths. *

* When dealing with filenames you can hit problems when moving from a Windows * based development machine to a Unix based production machine. * This class aims to help avoid those problems. *

* Most methods on this class are designed to work the same on both Unix and Windows. * Those that don't include 'System', 'Unix' or 'Windows' in their name. *

* Most methods recognise both separators (forward and back), and both * sets of prefixes. See the javadoc of each method for details. *

* This class defines six components within a filename (example C:\dev\project\file.txt): *

    *
  • the prefix - C:\
    • *
  • the path - dev\project
    • *
  • the full path - C:\dev\project
    • *
  • the name - file.txt
    • *
  • the base name - file
    • *
  • the extension - txt
    • *
* Note that the path of a directory is the parent directory. *

* This class only supports Unix and Windows style names. Prefixes are matched as follows: * 

 * Windows:
 * a\b\c.txt           --> ""          --> relative
 * \a\b\c.txt          --> "\"         --> current drive absolute
 * C:a\b\c.txt         --> "C:"        --> drive relative
 * C:\a\b\c.txt        --> "C:\"       --> absolute
 * \\server\a\b\c.txt  --> "\\server\" --> UNC
 *
 * Unix:
 * a/b/c.txt           --> ""          --> relative
 * /a/b/c.txt          --> "/"         --> absolute
 * ~/a/b/c.txt         --> "~/"        --> current user absolute
 * ~                   --> "~"         --> current user root (no path)
 * ~user/a/b/c.txt     --> "~user/"    --> named user absolute
 * ~user               --> "~user"     --> named user root (no path)
 *
* Both prefix styles are matched always, irrespective of the machine that you are * currently running on. * *

*

Origin of code

*
    *
  • Commons Utils
    • *
  • Alexandria's FileUtils
    • *
  • Avalon Excalibur's IO
    • *
  • Tomcat
    • *
* * @author Kevin A. Burton * @author Scott Sanders * @author Daniel Rall * @author Christoph.Reck * @author Peter Donald * @author Jeff Turner * @author Matthew Hawthorne * @author Martin Cooper * @author Jeremias Maerki * @author Stephen Colebourne * @version $Id$ * @since Commons IO 1.1 */
javadoc_after:
/** * Utility class that provides methods to manipulate filenames and filepaths. *

* When dealing with filenames you can hit problems when moving from a Windows * based development machine to a Unix based production machine. * This class aims to help avoid those problems. *

* Most methods on this class are designed to work the same on both Unix and Windows. * Those that don't include 'System', 'Unix' or 'Windows' in their name. *

* Most methods recognise both separators (forward and back), and both * sets of prefixes. See the javadoc of each method for details. *

* This class defines six components within a filename (example C:\dev\project\file.txt): *

    *
  • the prefix - C:\
    • *
  • the path - dev\project\
    • *
  • the full path - C:\dev\project\
    • *
  • the name - file.txt
    • *
  • the base name - file
    • *
  • the extension - txt
    • *
* Note that this class works best if directory filenames end with a separator. * If you omit the last separator, it is impossible to determine if the filename * corresponds to a file or a directory. As a result, we have chosen to say * it corresponds to a file. *

* This class only supports Unix and Windows style names. * Prefixes are matched as follows: * 

 * Windows:
 * a\b\c.txt           --> ""          --> relative
 * \a\b\c.txt          --> "\"         --> current drive absolute
 * C:a\b\c.txt         --> "C:"        --> drive relative
 * C:\a\b\c.txt        --> "C:\"       --> absolute
 * \\server\a\b\c.txt  --> "\\server\" --> UNC
 *
 * Unix:
 * a/b/c.txt           --> ""          --> relative
 * /a/b/c.txt          --> "/"         --> absolute
 * ~/a/b/c.txt         --> "~/"        --> current user
 * ~                   --> "~/"        --> current user (slash added)
 * ~user/a/b/c.txt     --> "~user/"    --> named user
 * ~user               --> "~user/"    --> named user (slash added)
 *
* Both prefix styles are matched always, irrespective of the machine that you are * currently running on. *

*

Origin of code

*
    *
  • Commons Utils
    • *
  • Alexandria's FileUtils
    • *
  • Avalon Excalibur's IO
    • *
  • Tomcat
    • *
* * @author Kevin A. Burton * @author Scott Sanders * @author Daniel Rall * @author Christoph.Reck * @author Peter Donald * @author Jeff Turner * @author Matthew Hawthorne * @author Martin Cooper * @author Jeremias Maerki * @author Stephen Colebourne * @version $Id$ * @since Commons IO 1.1 */
